data.title: CK Hutchison Shares Fall After Panama Court Strikes Down Port Contract
data.description:
By Tracy Qu


CK Hutchison's shares fell after Panama's Supreme Court ruled against the Hong Kong conglomerate running two ports on the Panama Canal.

The share decline came after the court said in a brief statement that license terms granted to CK Hutchison to run the ports of Balboa on the Pacific Coast and Cristobal on the Atlantic side were unconstitutional.

CK Hutchinson's shares fell 1.3% in early trade Friday and extended losses to 4.4% at the midday break following the court decision. The benchmark Hang Seng Index was down 1.8% as of the lunch break.

CK Hutchison didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

The decision had been closely watched in Washington and Beijing, and is seen as a key step in their broader dispute over the Panama Canal. At least 5% of global trade flows through the Panama Canal.


Write to Tracy Qu at tracy.qu@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-30-26 0014ET

data.title: Hang Lung Properties' 2025 Profit Drops as Revenue Lags
Indonesia Stock Exchange CEO Quits in Wake of Market Rout
data.description:
By Amanda Lee


The Indonesia Stock Exchange's chief executive has resigned after a two-day selloff sparked by concerns about the investability of local securities.

Iman Rachman told reporters Friday that he is stepping down to take responsibility for the recent volatility.

Indonesia stocks rebounded in morning trade, continuing a roller-coaster ride started after index provider MSCI Inc. warned of a possible downgrade of the Southeast Asian market's status.

That sent stocks tumbling Wednesday, and the Jakarta Composite Index to its lowest levels since November. An 8% drop Thursday triggered a trading halt, before the index clawed back ground to finish 1.1% lower.

On Friday, it bounced back, rising 1.2%.

Rachman said he hopes that the market will keep progressing after his resignation.

The market chaos ensued after MSCI froze index-related changes for Indonesian stocks over what it said were significant investor concerns about company ownership structures. That added to simmering anxiety about fiscal overreach and economic headwinds.

Regulators have vowed to address the issues raised by MSCI.

The efforts will likely boost clarity and transparency in securities trading, DBS's Radhika Rao said, noting plans including a potential doubling in minimum free-float requirements for listed companies.

"We expect more affirmative steps to assuage concerns of the investor community and global index providers, while ironing out opacity of business structure and any remnant governance issues in the medium-term," Rao said.


Write to Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-30-26 0013ET

China's factory activity expected to stall at start of new year
data.description:
BEIJING, Jan 30 (Reuters) - China's factory activity likely stalled in the first month of 2026, a Reuters poll showed on Friday, as still sluggish domestic demand held back production.

The survey of 25 economists forecast the official purchasing managers' index (PMI) would dip to 50.0 in January from 50.1 in December. The data is due on Saturday.

The projection follows a surprise recovery in December, when China's vast manufacturing sector snapped a record eight-month contraction streak.

The headline manufacturing PMI likely benefited from this year's unusually late Lunar New Year holidays, analysts at Mizuho Securities said.

"With the holiday landing in late February, many migrant workers may not return to their jobs until mid-March, prompting factories to front-load their manufacturing activity to the beginning of the year," Mizuho said.

Factory activity could face more pressure next month when holiday travel gets into full swing. The Lunar New Year holiday started in late January last year.

China's economy grew 5% in 2025, hitting Beijing's official target, with exports playing a heavy-lifter despite U.S. tariff pressures. Growth, however, has remained uneven. Retail sales weakened further in the final quarter, dragging fourth-quarter GDP growth to a three-year low.

Policymakers have vowed to make boosting domestic demand their top priority this year. The government front-loaded 62.5 billion yuan ($8.99 billion) from ultra-long special treasury bond funds to support its scheme offering consumers subsidies to replace a range of products from home appliances to smartphones.

But persistent weakness in the property market, a major source of household wealth, would mean authorities have their work cut out to persuade consumers to open up their wallets. 

Subdued demand at home has also pushed manufacturers to slash prices to defend market share, intensifying deflationary pressures and weighing on corporate earnings.

Beijing has stepped in to rein in excessive price wars under its so-called "anti-involution" crackdown. The crackdown helped bolster the bottom line for companies.

Industrial profit rose last year for the first time since 2021, and grew 5.3% in December from a year earlier, reversing a 13.1% decline in November.

China is likely to set this year's official growth target between 4.5% and 5%, the South China Morning Post reported, as policymakers take a cautious approach to stimulus with a stock market bubble on their minds.

Analysts polled by Reuters forecast the private sector RatingDog PMI to stand at 50.3, up from 50.1 a month prior. The data will be released on February 2. 

($1 = 6.9489 Chinese yuan)

(Reporting by Ethan Wang and Ryan Woo; Polling by Pranoy Krishna in Bengaluru and Jing Wang in Shanghai; Editing by Sam Holmes)

Low risk of Nipah virus spread beyond India, says WHO
data.description:
HYDERABAD, Jan 30 (Reuters) - The risk of the deadly Nipah virus spreading from India is low, the World Health Organisation said on Friday, adding that it does not recommend any travel or trade restrictions after the South Asian nation reported two cases of the virus infection.

(Reporting by Rishika Sadam; Editing by Tom Hogue)

By Rishika Sadam

data.title: Japan carefully considering implications of consumption tax suspension, finance minister says
data.description:
TOKYO, Jan 30 (Reuters) - Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is carefully considering the implications for fiscal sustainability of a consumption tax suspension and is consulting with the Finance Ministry, Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Friday.

Takaichi has proposed halting the 8% consumption tax on food and drinks for a period of two years, which precipitated a jump in government bond yields and a decline in the yen on market fears it could require funding through more debt issuance.

Speaking at a campaign event in support of a lower house election candidate in Tokyo, Katayama said that she had explained the justification for the tax cut at the World Economic Forum's annual gathering in Davos earlier this month and that current market conditions had stabilised.

(Reporting by Tamiyuki Kihara; Writing by Anton Bridge; Editing by Jamie Freed)
